title: Voting with Neighbors: kNN Basics
concept: Classify a new point by looking at the k closest points and letting them vote on the correct category.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is a machine learning pipeline?

options.text: A sequence of connected steps—from raw data to evaluation—that automates the machine learning process.
A literal physical pipe used to cool down hot servers.
A method that only works for recommendation systems.

exercises.explain: Perfect! A pipeline connects data prep, training, and evaluation into a smooth, automated workflow.

cards.title: Birds of a feather
cards.body: If you walk into a park and see a bird you don't recognize, how do you guess what kind it is? You might look at the birds closest to it. If it's sitting next to three ducks, it's probably a duck! This simple instinct is the heart of one of the friendliest AI algorithms.

cards.title: Meet kNN
cards.body: k-Nearest Neighbors (kNN) is a classification algorithm that predicts the category of a new data point by looking at the k closest data points (neighbors) and letting them vote on what the new point should be.

cards.title: Neighborhood voting
cards.body: Imagine a new family moves into a neighborhood. To decide which local school team to support (Red vs. Blue), they ask their 5 closest neighbors. If 4 support Red and 1 supports Blue, the new family "votes" with the majority and joins the Red team.

cards.title: Fruit classification
cards.body: Suppose you map fruits on a grid by their weight and sweetness. You place a mystery fruit. If you look at its 3 closest neighbors (k = 3) on the map and find 2 apples and 1 orange, the model classifies the mystery fruit as an apple!

cards.title: The lazy learner
cards.body: You might think every AI needs hours of training to build a complex mathematical model. But kNN is a "lazy learner"! It doesn't build a model. It just stores the data. It only does the work of finding neighbors when you ask it to classify a new point.

cards.title: How voting works
cards.body: See how kNN determines a class by looking at its neighbors:

left.text:
Look at the k closest points
e.g. k = 3 neighbors on the grid

right.text:
Majority wins!
If 2 are Blue and 1 is Red, class is Blue.

exercises.prompt: If we set k = 5, how does the kNN algorithm classify a new data point?

options.text: It looks at the 5 closest data points and assigns the most common category among them.
It randomly chooses 5 data points from the entire dataset.
It calculates the average of all points in the dataset.

exercises.explain: Exactly! The 'k' in kNN is the number of closest neighbors that get a vote. If k = 5, it uses the 5 closest points.

exercises.prompt: True or False: kNN goes through a long, complex training process to build mathematical formulas before it can make predictions.
exercises.explain: False! kNN is a 'lazy learner'. It simply stores the training data and does the work of measuring distances only when making a prediction.

exercises.sentence: The 'k' in kNN stands for the number of nearest ___ that get to vote on a new point's category.
exercises.explain: The 'k' stands for the number of closest neighbors used to make the decision.

exercises.prompt: Which of these describes a kNN voting process?

options.text: A mystery song is classified as 'Jazz' because 4 out of its 5 closest sounding songs are Jazz.
A song is classified as 'Rock' because the word 'guitar' appears 10 times in its lyrics.
A song is classified as 'Pop' because a human expert listened to it and labeled it.

exercises.explain: Yes! Classifying based on the majority label of the closest neighbors is the definition of kNN voting.

exercises.prompt: Imagine you are building a toy-sorting robot. You show it a mystery toy. Its 3 closest neighbors on the shelf are a Red Ball, a Red Block, and a Blue Block. If k = 3, what will the robot classify the mystery toy as?

options.text: A Block, because 2 out of the 3 nearest neighbors are blocks.
A Red toy, because color is the only feature that matters.
A Ball, because it's the closest overall neighbor.
A Blue toy, because it's the newest color.

exercises.explain: Spot on! The categories are 'Ball' vs. 'Block'. Since 2 neighbors are Blocks and only 1 is a Ball, 'Block' wins the majority vote. kNN votes based on category labels!
